html:
XV

One by One

You could smell the spring.

вАЬвАКвАЩTainвАЩt gwine be warm foвАЩ weeks yet!вАЭ Hager said.

Nevertheless, you could smell the spring. Little boys were already running in the streets without their overcoats, and the groundhog had seen its shadow. Snow remained in the fence corners, but it had melted on the roofs. The yards were wet and muddy, but no longer white.

It was a sunny afternoon in late March that a letter came. On his last delivery the mailman stopped, dropped it in the boxвБ†вАФand Sandy saw him. It was addressed to his mother and he knew it must be from Jimboy.

вАЬGo on anвАЩ take it to her,вАЭ his grandma said, as soon as she saw the boy coming with it in his hand. вАЬI knows thatвАЩs what you want to do. Go on anвАЩ take it.вАЭ And she bent over her ironing again.

Sandy ran almost all the way to Mrs.¬†RiceвАЩs, dropping the letter more than once on the muddy sidewalk, so excited he did not think to put it in his pocket. Into the big yard and around to the white ladyвАЩs back door he spedвБ†вАФand it was locked! He knocked loudly for a long time, and finally an upper window opened and Annjee, a dust-rag around her head, looked down, squinting in the sunlight.

вАЬWhoвАЩs there?вАЭ she called stridently, thinking of some peddler or belated tradesman for whom she did not wish to stop her cleaning.

Sandy pantingly held up the letter and was about to say something when the window closed with a bang. He could hear his mother almost falling down the back stairs, she was coming so fast. Then the key turned swiftly in the lock, the door opened, and, without closing it, Annjee took the letter from him and tore it open where she stood.

вАЬItвАЩs from Jimboy!вАЭ

Sandy stood on the steps looking at his mother, her bosom heaving, her sleeves rolled up, and the white cloth tied about her head, doubly white against her dark-brown face.

вАЬHeвАЩs in Detroit, it says.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Umn! I ainвАЩt never seen him write such a long letter. вАШI had a hard time this winter till I landed here,вАЩ it says, вАШbut things look pretty good now, and there is lots of building going on and plenty of work opening up in the automobile plantsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ a mighty lot of colored folks hereвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ hope you and Sandy been well. Sorry couldnвАЩt send you nothing Xmas, but I was in St.¬†Paul broke.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Kiss my son for me.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Tell ma hello even if she donвАЩt want to hear it. Your loving husband, Jimboy Rodgers.вАЩвАКвАЭ

Annjee did her best to hold the letter with one hand and pick up Sandy with the other, but he had grown considerably during the winter and she was still a little weak from her illness; so she bent down to his level and kissed him several times before she reread the letter.

вАЬFrom your daddy!вАЭ she said. вАЬUmn-mn.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Come on in here and warm yourself. Lemme see what he says again!вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She lighted the gas oven in the white kitchen and sat down in front of it with her letter, forgetting the clock and the approaching time for Mrs.¬†RiceвАЩs dinner, forgetting everything. вАЬA letter from my daddy! From my far-off sugar-daddy!вАЭ

вАЬFrom my daddy,вАЭ corrected Sandy.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬSay, gimme a nickel to buy some marbles, mama. I wanta go play.вАЭ

Without taking her eyes from the precious note Annjee fumbled in her apron and found a coin. вАЬTake it and go on!вАЭ she said.

It was a dime. Sandy skipped around the house and down the street in the chilly sunshine. He decided to stop at BusterвАЩs for a while before going home, since he had to pass there anyway, and he found his friend in the house trying to carve boats from clothespins with a rusty jackknife.

BusterвАЩs mother was a seamstress, and, after opening the front door and greeting Sandy with a cheery вАЬHello,вАЭ she returned to her machine and a friend who was calling on her. She was a tall young light-mulatto woman, with skin like old ivory. Maybe that was why Buster was so white. But her husband was a black man who worked on the cityвАЩs garbage-trucks and was active politically when election time came, getting colored men to vote Republican. Everybody said he made lots of money, but that he wasnвАЩt really BusterвАЩs father.

The golden-haired child gave Sandy a butcher-knife and together they whacked at the clothespins. You could hear the two women talking plainly in the little sewing-room, where the machine ran between snatches of conversation.

вАЬYes,вАЭ BusterвАЩs mother was saying, вАЬI have the hardest time keeping that boy colored! He goes on just like he was white. Do you know what he did last week? Cut all the blossoms off my geranium plants here in the house, took them to school, and gave them to Dorothy Marlow, in his grade. And you know who Dorothy is, donвАЩt you? Senator MarlowвАЩs daughter!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I said: вАШBuster, if you ever cut my flowers to carry to any little girl again, IвАЩll punish you severely, but if you cut them to carry to little white girls, I donвАЩt know what IвАЩll do with you.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ DonвАЩt you know they hang colored boys for things like that?вАЩ I wanted to scare himвБ†вАФbecause you know there might be trouble even among kids in school over such things.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But I had to laugh.вАЭ

Her friend laughed too. вАЬHeвАЩs a hot one, taking flowers to the women already, and a white girl at that! YouвАЩve got a fast-working son, Elvira, I must say.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But, do you know, when you first moved here and I saw you and the boy going in and out, I thought sure you were both white folks. I didnвАЩt know you was colored till my husband said: вАШThatвАЩs EddieвАЩs wife!вАЩ You-all sure looked white to me.вАЭ

The machine started to whir, making the conversation inaudible for a few minutes, and when Sandy caught their words again, they were talking about the ElksвАЩ clubhouse that the colored people were planning to build.

вАЬCan you go out?вАЭ Sandy demanded of Buster, sure they were making no headway with the tough clothespins and dull knives.

вАЬMaybe,вАЭ said Buster. вАЬIвАЩll go see.вАЭ And he went into the other room and asked his mother.

вАЬPut on your overcoat,вАЭ she commanded. вАЬItвАЩs not summer yet. And be back in here before dark.вАЭ

вАЬAll right, Vira,вАЭ the child said.

The two children went to Mrs.¬†RumfordвАЩs shop on the corner and bought three centsвАЩ worth of candy and seven centsвАЩ worth of peewees with which to play marbles when it got warm. Then Sandy walked back past BusterвАЩs house with him and they played for a while in the street before Sandy turned to run home.

Aunt Hager was making mush for supper. She sent him to the store for a pint bottle of milk as soon as he arrived, but he forgot to take the bottle and had to come back for it.

вАЬYouвАЩd forget yoвАЩ head if it wasnвАЩt tied to you!вАЭ the old woman reminded him.

They were just finishing supper when Annjee got home with two chocolate eclairs in her coat-pocket, mashed together against JimboyвАЩs letter.

вАЬHuh! IвАЩm crazy!вАЭ she said, running her hand down into the sticky mess. вАЬBut listen, ma! HeвАЩs got a job and is doing well in Detroit, Jimboy says.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And IвАЩm going to him!вАЭ

вАЬYou what?вАЭ Hager gasped, dropping her spoon in her mush-bowl. вАЬWhat you sayinвАЩ?вАЭ

вАЬI said IвАЩm going to him, ma! I got to!вАЭ Annjee stood with her coat and hat still on, holding the sticky letter. вАЬIвАЩm going where my heart is, ma!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Oh, not today.вАЭ She put her arms around her motherвАЩs neck. вАЬI donвАЩt mean today, mama, nor next week. I got to save some money first. I only got a little now. But I mean IвАЩm going to him soonвАЩs I can. I canвАЩt help it, maвБ†вАФI love him!вАЭ

вАЬLawd, is you foolish?вАЭ cried Hager. вАЬWhatвАЩs you gwine do with this chile, trapesinвАЩ round after Jimboy? What you gwine do if he leaves you in Detroiter or wherever he are? What you gwine do then? You loves him! Huh!вАЭ

вАЬBut he ainвАЩt gonna leave me in Detroit, вАЩcause IвАЩm going with him everywhere he goes,вАЭ she said, her eyes shining. вАЬHe ainвАЩt gonna leave me no more!вАЭ

вАЬAnвАЩ Sandy?вАЭ

вАЬCouldnвАЩt he stay with you, mama? And then maybe weвАЩd come back here and live, Jimboy and me, some time, when we get a little money ahead, and could pay off the mortgage on the house.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But there ainвАЩt no use arguing, mama, I got to go!вАЭ

Hager had never seen Annjee so positive before; she sat speechless, looking at the bowl of mush.

вАЬI got to go where it ainвАЩt lonesome and where I ainвАЩt unhappyвБ†вАФand thatвАЩs where Jimboy is! I got to go soon as I can.вАЭ

Hager rose to put some water on the stove to heat for the dishes.

вАЬOne by one you leaves meвБ†вАФTempy, then Harriett, then you,вАЭ she said. вАЬBut SandyвАЩs gonna stick by me, ainвАЩt you, son? He ainвАЩt gwine leave his grandma.вАЭ

The youngster looked at Hager, moving slowly about the kitchen putting away the supper things.

вАЬAnd IвАЩs gwine to make a fine man out oвАЩ you, Sandy. IвАЩs gwine raise one chile right yet, if de Lawd lets me liveвБ†вАФjust one chile right!вАЭ she murmured.

That night the March wind began to blow and the windowpanes rattled. Sandy woke up in the dark, lying close and warm beside his mother. When he went back to sleep again, he dreamed that his Aunt TempyвАЩs Christmas book had been turned into a chariot, and that he was riding through the sky with Tempy standing very dignifiedly beside him as he drove. And he couldnвАЩt see anybody down on earth, not even Hager.

When his mother rolled out at six oвАЩclock to go to work, he woke up again, and while she dressed, he lay watching his breath curl mistily upwards in the cold room. Outside the window it was bleak and grey and the March wind, humming through the leafless branches of the trees, blew terrifically. He heard Aunt Hager in the kitchen poking at the stove, making up a blaze to start the coffee boiling. Then the front door closed when his mother went out and, as the door slammed, the wind howled fiercely. It was nice and warm in bed, so he lay under the heavy quilts half dreaming, half thinking, until his grandmother shook him to get up. And many were the queer, dream-drowsy thoughts that floated through his mindвБ†вАФnot only that morning, but almost every morning while he lay beneath the warm quilts until Hager had called him three or four times to get ready for school.

He wondered sometimes whether if he washed and washed his face and hands, he would ever be white. Someone had told him once that blackness was only skin-deep.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And would he ever have a big house with electric lights in it, like his Aunt TempyвБ†вАФbut it was mostly white people who had such fine things, and they were mean to colored.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Some white folks were nice, though. Earl was nice at school, but not the little boys across the street, who called him вАЬniggerвАЭ every dayвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and not Mrs.¬†Rice, who scolded his mother.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Aunt Harrie didnвАЩt like any white folks at all.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But Jesus was white and wore a long, white robe, like a womanвАЩs, on the Sunday-school cards.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Once Jimmy Lane said: вАЬGod damn JesusвАЭ when the teacher scolded him for not knowing his Bible lesson. He said it out loud in church, too, and the church didnвАЩt fall down on him, as Sandy thought it might.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Grandma said it was a sin to cuss and swear, but all the fellows at school sworeвБ†вАФand Jimboy did, too. But every time Sandy said вАЬGod damn,вАЭ he felt bad, because Aunt Hager said God was mighty good and it was wrong to take His name in vain. But he would like to learn to say вАЬGod damnвАЭ without feeling anything like most boys said itвБ†вАФjust вАЬGod damn!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ God damn!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ God damn!вАЭ without being ashamed of himself.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The Lord never seemed to notice, anyhow.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And when he got big, he wanted to travel like Jimboy. He wanted to be a railroad engineer, but Harriett had said there werenвАЩt any colored engineers on trains.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What would he be, then? Maybe a doctor; but it was more fun being an engineer and travelling far away.

Sandy wished Annjee would take him with her when she went to join JimboyвБ†вАФbut then Aunt Hager would be all by herself, and grandma was so nice to him he would hate to leave her alone. Who would cut wood for her then?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But when he got big, he would go to Detroit. And maybe New York, too, where his geography said they had the tallest buildings in the world, and trains that ran under the river.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He wondered if there were any colored people in New York.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ How ugly African colored folks looked in the geographyвБ†вАФwith bushy heads and wild eyes! Aunt Hager said her mother was an African, but she wasnвАЩt ugly and wild; neither was Aunt Hager; neither was little dark Willie-Mae, and they were all black like Africans.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And Reverend Braswell was as black as ink, but he knew God.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ God didnвАЩt care if people were black, did He?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What was God? Was He a man or a lamb or what? BusterвАЩs mother said God was a light, but Aunt Hager said He was a King and had a throne and wore a crownвБ†вАФshe intended to sit down by His side by and by.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Was BusterвАЩs father white? Buster was white and colored both. But he didnвАЩt look like he was colored. What made Buster not colored?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And what made girls different from boys?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Once when they were playing house, Willie-Mae told him how girls were different from boys, but they didnвАЩt know why. Now Willie-Mae was in the seventh grade and had hard little breasts that stuck out sharp-like, and Jimmy Lane said dirty things about Willie-Mae.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Once he asked his mother what his navel was for and she said, вАЬLayovers to catch meddlers.вАЭ What did that mean?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And how come ladies got sick and stayed in bed when they had babies? Where did babies come from, anyhow? Not from storksвБ†вАФa fairy-story like Santa Claus.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Did God love people who told fairy-stories and lied to kids about storks and Santa Claus?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Santa Claus was no good, anyhow! God damn Santa Claus for not bringing him the sled he wanted Christmas! It was all a lie about Santa Claus!

The sound of Hager pouring coal on the fire and dragging her washtubs across the kitchen floor to get ready for work broke in on SandyвАЩs drowsy half-dreams, and as he rolled over in bed, his grandmother, hearing the springs creak, called loudly: вАЬYou Sandy! Get up from there! ItвАЩs seven and past! You want to be late gettinвАЩ to yoвАЩ school?вАЭ

вАЬYesвАЩm, IвАЩm coming, grandma!вАЭ he said under the quilts. вАЬBut itвАЩs cold in here.вАЭ

вАЬYou knows you donвАЩt dress in yonder! Bring them clothes on out behind this stove, sir.вАЭ

вАЬYesвАЩm.вАЭ So with a kick of the feet his covers went flying back and Sandy ran to the warmth of the little kitchen, where he dressed, washed, and ate. Then he yelled for Willie-MaeвБ†вАФwhen he felt like itвБ†вАФor else went on to school without her, joining some of the boys on the way.

So spring was coming and Annjee worked diligently at Mrs.¬†RiceвАЩs day after day. Often she did something extra for Mrs.¬†RiceвАЩs sister and her childrenвБ†вАФpressed a shirtwaist or ironed some stockingsвБ†вАФand so added a few quarters or maybe even a dollar to her weekly wages, all of which she saved to help carry her to Jimboy in Detroit.

For ten years she had been cooking, washing, ironing, scrubbingвБ†вАФand for what? For only the few weeks in a year, or a half-year, when Jimboy would come home from some strange place and take her in his strong arms and kiss her and murmur: вАЬAnnjee, baby!вАЭ ThatвАЩs what she had been working forвБ†вАФthen the dreary months were as nothing, and the hard years faded away. But now he had been gone all winter, and, from his letter, he might not come back soon, because he said Detroit was a fine place for colored folks.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But StantonвБ†вАФwell, Annjee thought there must surely be better towns, where a woman wouldnвАЩt have to work so hard to live.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And where Jimboy was.

So before the first buds opened on the apple-tree in the backyard, Annjee had gone to Detroit, leaving Sandy behind with his grandmother. And when the apple-blossoms came in full bloom, there was no one living in the little house but a grey-headed old woman and her grandchild.

вАЬOne by one they leaves you,вАЭ Hager said slowly. вАЬOne by one yoвАЩ chillen goes.вАЭ
